summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'distribution-free test'?

Nonparametric tests are called 'distribution-free' because they do not assume the data come from any particular probability distribution family.

What is the 'sign test'?

The sign test uses only the direction of differences (positive or negative), not their magnitude. It is one of the simplest nonparametric tests and is very robust but less powerful than the Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

What is the 'asymptotic relative efficiency' of a nonparametric test?

Asymptotic relative efficiency compares nonparametric to parametric tests. For example, the Mann-Whitney test has about 95.5% efficiency relative to the t-test under normality, meaning it needs only slightly more data to achieve the same power.

What is the 'Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test'?

The Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test and the Mann-Whitney U Test are mathematically equivalent tests for comparing two independent groups using ranked data.

What is the key advantage of Spearman's r_s over Pearson's r?

Spearman's r_s ranks both variables and computes the Pearson correlation on the ranks. This captures monotonic (not just linear) relationships and is resistant to outliers and non-normality.

A researcher has 8 patients' blood pressure readings before and after a new medication. The sample is too small to verify normality. She uses the sign test and finds 7 out of 8 patients had lower blood pressure after treatment. Under H-null (no effect), what is the probability of 7 or more successes out of 8?

Under H-null, each difference is equally likely to be + or - (p = 0.5). P(X >= 7) = P(X=7) + P(X=8) = C(8,7)(0.5)^8 + C(8,8)(0.5)^8 = (8 + 1)/256 = 9/256 = 0.035.

A dataset has 12 paired observations. The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test yields a test statistic of W = 10 with a critical value of 14 for alpha = 0.05 (two-tailed, where W must be <= critical value to reject). What is the conclusion?

For the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test, smaller values of W provide evidence against H-null. Since W = 10 <= 14 (the critical value), we reject H-null at alpha = 0.05.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Nonparametric tests are robust to outliers because they use ranks rather than raw values. An extreme outlier gets the highest or lowest rank, but its extreme magnitude does not disproportionately affect the test statistic.

Three independent groups with heavily skewed data and outliers call for the Kruskal-Wallis test, which is the nonparametric alternative to one-way ANOVA and is robust to non-normality.

The extreme outlier (98) would inflate the control group mean and standard deviation, distorting a t-test. The Mann-Whitney U Test uses ranks, so the outlier simply receives the highest rank without disproportionately affecting the result.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the key advantage of Spearman's r_s over Pearson's r?

Spearman's r_s ranks both variables and computes the Pearson correlation on the ranks. This captures monotonic (not just linear) relationships and is resistant to outliers and non-normality.

Example: A biologist studies the relationship between tree age (years) and trunk diameter (cm) for 30 trees. Both variables are measured but the relationship appears curved (monotonic but not linear). Which correlation measure is better?

Spearman's r_s captures monotonic relationships regardless of whether the relationship is linear. Since tree age and diameter increase together but not at a constant rate, Spearman's r_s is more appropriate than Pearson's r.

Example: A psychologist wants to test whether anxiety scores differ between a control group and a treatment group. The control group (n = 12) has scores with a severe outlier of 98 (all others range 15-45). The treatment group (n = 14) has normally distributed scores ranging 10-35. Which test should she use and why?

The extreme outlier (98) would inflate the control group mean and standard deviation, distorting a t-test. The Mann-Whitney U Test uses ranks, so the outlier simply receives the highest rank without disproportionately affecting the result.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

If a distribution is heavily right-skewed with a small sample, the best approach is:

With small n and clear non-normality, nonparametric methods are preferred because the t-test's normality assumption is not met, and the CLT cannot compensate with such a small sample.

A marketing team wants to compare customer satisfaction ratings (on a 5-point Likert scale) between online and in-store shoppers. The data are ordinal and the distributions are highly skewed. With 25 online and 30 in-store shoppers, which test is most appropriate

Two independent groups, ordinal data, and non-normal distributions all point to the Mann-Whitney U Test as the appropriate choice.

A researcher measures income (in dollars) for three education groups (high school, bachelor's, graduate). Income distributions are heavily right-skewed with outliers. Each group has about 20 participants. Which test should the researcher use

Three independent groups with heavily skewed data and outliers call for the Kruskal-Wallis test, which is the nonparametric alternative to one-way ANOVA and is robust to non-normality.
